title: The body freeze response is not laziness it is protection
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
You called it laziness. I called it shame.

I used to think I was broken. That's the honest truth. There were days when I couldn't get out of bed, couldn't answer a text, couldn't even feed myself properly. And I'd lie there, staring at the ceiling, and call myself every name in the book. Lazy. Worthless. A waste of space.

Here's the thing. I wasn't lazy. I was frozen.

My nervous system had hit a wall. It had been running on empty for years, decades maybe, and it finally said "no more." Not in a dramatic way. Not with a crash or a breakdown that anyone could see. Just a quiet shutdown. A slow dimming of the lights. A body that refused to move because moving had become too dangerous.

Sound familiar? Right?

Let's talk about what's actually happening when you can't get started. When your legs feel like they're filled with concrete. When your brain says "just do the thing" but your body says "absolutely not." When you watch yourself fail to act and feel that familiar wave of self-hatred wash over you.

This isn't a character flaw. It's a survival response.

The freeze response is ancient wisdom

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades mapping out how our nervous system actually works. Not how we think it should work, but how it really works. And what he found changed everything I thought I knew about my own stuckness.

Here's the short version. Your nervous system has three main states. Ventral vagal is your social engagement state, where you feel safe and connected. Sympathetic is your fight or flight state, where you mobilize to deal with threats. And dorsal vagal is your freeze state, where your system shuts down to conserve energy and protect you from overwhelming danger.

Stay with me here. This matters.

Most of us think of freeze as the last resort. The thing that happens when fight and flight fail. And that's true in some cases. But here's what I've learned from my own body and from reading people like Peter Levine, who wrote "Waking the Tiger" about trauma and the body. Freeze isn't just a failure of other responses. It's an intelligent, adaptive, life-saving strategy.

Think about it. When a prey animal is caught by a predator, what happens? If it can't fight and can't flee, it plays dead. Its nervous system drops into a deep freeze. Heart rate slows. Breathing becomes shallow. Pain signals get muted. The animal goes limp. And sometimes, the predator loses interest. The animal survives.

Your body learned this trick too. Not because you're weak. Because you're smart.

When you grew up in an environment that was chronically unsafe, unpredictable, or overwhelming, your nervous system learned that mobilization was dangerous. Moving, speaking, taking action, those things got you hurt. So your body developed a different strategy. Stay still. Stay small. Stay invisible. Survive.

That's not laziness. That's protection.

What freeze looks like in daily life

It doesn't always look like lying in bed all day. Sometimes freeze looks like scrolling through your phone for three hours when you have work to do. Sometimes it looks like zoning out during a conversation. Sometimes it looks like procrastination so severe that it ruins your relationships or your career.

I remember a period in my twenties when I couldn't make a single phone call. Not one. I needed to call the dentist, the bank, my landlord, and I just couldn't. I'd write the number down. I'd stare at it. I'd feel this invisible wall between me and the phone. And I'd hate myself for being so pathetic.

But I wasn't pathetic. I was frozen. My nervous system had learned that reaching out, making myself known, asking for something, those things were dangerous. And it was doing its job. It was keeping me safe.

Janina Fisher, who trained under Bessel van der Kolk and specializes in trauma treatment, talks about this constantly. She says that the parts of us that freeze are often the parts that saved our lives. They're not our enemies. They're our protectors. They just don't know that the danger has passed.

And honestly, sometimes the danger hasn't passed. Sometimes we're still in survival mode because we're still in survival conditions. Not everyone who freezes has a trauma history. Sometimes freeze is a perfectly reasonable response to an overwhelming present.

Either way, it's not your fault. And it's not laziness.

How to work with freeze instead of fighting it

Here's where most advice goes wrong. The wellness industry will tell you to push through. To just do it. To make a to-do list and check things off. To use willpower and discipline to overcome your resistance.

That advice works for people whose nervous systems are regulated. It works for people who have enough safety in their bodies to mobilize. But if you're in freeze, pushing through is like telling someone with a broken leg to walk it off. It's not just unhelpful. It's harmful.

What actually helps is gentleness. Slowness. Curiosity. And most of all, safety.

I started by noticing what freeze felt like in my body. Not judging it. Just noticing. The heaviness in my chest. The shallow breath. The sense of being behind glass, watching myself from far away. I stopped calling it laziness and started calling it what it was. Protection.

Then I started working with my breath. Not to force myself into action, but to send a signal of safety to my nervous system. A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is the Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). It extends your exhale naturally, which activates the vagus nerve and tells your body it's okay to come out of freeze. I use it when I feel stuck, and it helps me move from dorsal vagal into ventral vagal without force.

I also started paying attention to what my body needed nutritionally. One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium is involved in over 300 biochemical reactions in your body, including the regulation of your stress response. And glycinate form is specifically calming. It's not a magic bullet, but it can help your system find its way back to regulation.

Movement also matters, but not in the way you think. I don't mean high intensity interval training or pushing yourself to run five miles. I mean gentle, pendular movement that releases stored tension. A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is the TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). Rolling out your back, your hips, your legs, it can help your body process the stuck energy that freeze leaves behind. Go slow. Breathe. Let your body tell you what it needs.

And finally, I read. I read everything I could find about the nervous system, about trauma, about why my body did what it did. A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link) by Gabor Mate. He writes about how chronic stress and suppressed emotion show up as physical disease. Reading that book was like looking in a mirror. It helped me stop blaming myself and start understanding myself.

The shame is the real prison

Here's what I want you to take away from this. The freeze response itself isn't the problem. It's a natural, intelligent, protective response. The problem is what we tell ourselves about it. The shame. The self-hatred. The belief that we're broken or lazy or worthless.

That shame keeps us stuck. It activates our nervous system even more. It tells our body "see, I'm still in danger, the threat is still here." And the freeze deepens.

Breaking the cycle starts with changing the story. Not with forcing yourself to do things you can't do. Not with more discipline or more structure or more self-criticism. But with compassion. With curiosity. With the radical act of saying to yourself "I am not lazy. I am protecting myself. And I can learn new ways to feel safe."

I'm not going to tell you that recovery is linear or easy. It's not. Some days I still freeze. Some days I still feel that heaviness in my chest and that voice in my head calling me names. But I know now what's actually happening. And that knowledge changes everything.

Your body is not your enemy. It's your oldest, most loyal protector. It learned to freeze because that's what kept you alive. And now, slowly, gently, you can teach it that there's another way.

Start by noticing. Start by breathing. Start by being kind to the part of you that's scared to move. That part deserves gratitude, not punishment.

Wild, right? That the thing you hate most about yourself might actually be the thing that saved your life.

Sit with that.
